title: Biometric Recognition
html:
Biometric Recognition

Automatic recognition of an individual based on physiological or behavioral characteristics — fingerprint, iris, face, voice, palmprint, retina, signature, keystroke dynamics, even odor. The category Yahir studied formally in spring 2025 OSU coursework via the canonical Jain/Pankanti/Prabhakar IEEE Xplore survey, screenshotted across three sessions on April 27 (2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 21 pm e4e5aad8, 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 40 38 pm 56e50e24, 2025 04 27 screenshot 2025 04 27 at 7 41 04 pm 5f2b64af). The structural distinction the survey insists on: knowledge-based auth (passwords) and token-based auth (cards) use surrogates for identity; only biometrics binds the credential to the person. But biometrics alone isn't enough either — sound systems combine biometric + nonbiometric components.

The modality zoo in the first screenshot ranks each method on accuracy, spoofability, and user-friction: iris is distinctive even between identical twins and trivial to detect designer contact lenses; palmprint is more distinctive than fingerprint but bulkier; retinal scan is the most secure and the most invasive (and leaks medical info — hypertension shows up); voice is the only modality that fits tele-banking; signature is legally accepted but defeatable by pro forgers; keystroke dynamics is behavioral, monitorable unobtrusively. The closing line of that page: "each biometric technique is admissible and there is no optimal biometric characteristic." The right answer depends on the application domain — the technical version of asking the right question.

The math page is where the concept becomes load-bearing. FMR (False Match Rate) scales linearly with database size when you go from verification (1:1) to identification (1:N) — a 0.001% verification FMR becomes a 10% identification FMR at 10,000 users. A hacker with all ten fingers gets in. This is the structural reason "let's just put biometrics on everything" doesn't survive contact with arithmetic, and it's why multimodal biometrics is the only viable path for large-N identification (face + fingerprint, multi-finger, etc.). The ethics page closes with the line that aged extremely well: "It is too early to predict where and how biometric technology would evolve and get embedded in which applications. But it is certain that biometric-based recognition will have a profound influence on the way we conduct our daily business" — written 22 years ago.

The connection to Rankett is structural rather than topical. The "semi-automatic" framing the survey uses (system narrows the candidates, human decides) is the same shape as how Yahir thinks about ai search optimization and the ai visibility engine — the AI narrows, the human (or the agency client) closes. Biometric negative recognition (the system doesn't remember you if you're not in the criminal database) and AI-narrowed lead lists rhyme. The biometrics survey is the academic spine of the broader Yahir security/identity intuition; if any future product touches identity verification, KYC, deepfake defense, or AI-vs-human auth, it inherits this paper's frame.
